turns.text: editmore_vertUserYou are analyzing a YouTube video transcript. Extract and structure the following information as valid JSON only (no markdown, no extra text).Return ONLY this JSON structure:{"summary": "2-3 sentence summary of the video content","keyPoints": ["point 1", "point 2", ...],"aiConcepts": ["AI concept 1", "AI concept 2", ...],"technicalDetails": ["tool/framework 1", "implementation detail", ...],"visualContextFlags": [{"timestamp": 120, "reason": "Code demo", "context": "Shows Python implementation"}]}If the video is not about AI/tech, set aiConcepts and technicalDetails to empty arrays but still extract keyPoints.TRANSCRIPT:I just added a human-like AI agent to my Next.js app using only one reactor. No complex setup, no back-end required, just pure conversational AI. This one single line gives you access to a fully featured AI agent that can listen to user voice input in real time, process natural language, respond with human-like speech with low latency, and handle conversational state automatically. Let me show you how this works. For this demo, I've created a wizard which will guide the user with their quest. Let's start the conversation. Greetings, traveler. How can I assist you on your quest? Greetings, wizard. May I know your name? You may call me Callum. I am a wizard and I am here to help you on your quest. Remember, your goal is to collect 11 gemstones scattered throughout Labzonia. I can help you find them and overcome any obstacles you may-- Well met, Callum. Sorry for interrupting you, but I do need to get a move on. People are waiting for me to finish this demo. Can you tell me more about the weapons that I need to complete this quest? Ah, yes, the weapons. You will need a variety of weapons to complete your quest, traveler. Each gemstone is guarded by a different type of creature, and you will need the right weapon to defeat them. For example, you will need a sword to fight the goblin, a bow and arrow to defeat the flying creatures, and a magic staff to defeat the wizards. You can find these weapons scattered throughout Labzonia, so keep your eyes peeled. Actually, sorry for interrupting you again, but I would also like to learn how to do magic. Of course, traveler. Magic is an essential part of this quest. I can teach you some basic spells to help-- The potential for this is just absolutely insane, and best of all, you can also add custom tools, as well as knowledge bases, to your agents as well. So, let's get started. Now, I don't want to spend too much time building the user interface, so I have created some boilerplate for you to get started. This way, we can simply focus on the SDK itself. In the description of the video, you will find a link to this GitHub repository. So, to get this code, simply click on "code", and then download the zip file, and then open the contents of that folder in your code editor. Let's install all the project dependencies by running npm install, or I'm actually going to use pnpm, but it's really up to you. Now, let's start the dev server by running npm run dev. Let's open this URL in the browser. Now, obviously, nothing happens when we click on "start conversation". We will be implementing all of this logic during the course of this video. And if we have a look at the code, we can go to the source directory. Then, if we have a look at the app directory and the page, there's really not a lot going on here. We've simply got a main tag with a heading, and the component that will be changing is this voice component, which we can find in the components folder. And if we open up this component, this simply contains the UI design for this voice component over here. I've simply gone ahead and created a few handler functions to start the conversation, to end the conversation, and to toggle mute. You can also implement a similar function for decreasing or increasing the agent's volume. We'll simply mute the volume entirely. And then we're simply using chat cn to create this very basic layout. And in this design, we have a button to mute the conversation. And within the main card content, we have another button to start the conversation. But as you can see, this is some very basic boilerplate. And now we'll have a look at adding our agent to this component. In order to create our agent, we will be using E11 Labs. At the time of recording this, the conversational AI assistants are actually free to use, as they currently are in the beta phase. So this is the perfect time to play with this technology, as this will become very popular in the next year. Go over to E11labs.io, or use the link in the description of this video to support my channel. Then sign up or sign into your account. We can then create our agent by going to the conversational section and then click on agents. Then let's click on create an AI agent. Here we can give our agent a name, like I'll just call mine Wizard demo. Then we can select to start from scratch or start with one of these templates, like a support agent, a math tutor, or a video game character. And we'll use the video game character. Let's click on create agent. We're not going to change much on this agent, but I do briefly want to show you that we can set things like the welcome message, the system prompt, we can swap out the LLM provider, and we can also add a custom knowledge base and tool calling. I've already created a separate video which goes into detail on creating these agents, along with using the knowledge base feature and providing tools to this agent. So definitely check out that other video if it's something you would be interested in. But for now, all we really want is to copy the agent ID and then back in Next.js, let's create a new file in the root directory, and let's call it .env.local. Then let's create a new variable called next_public_11labs_agent_id. This variable will be publicly available, hence the next_public prefix. But don't worry about other people abusing this though, as I will show you towards the end of the video how you can ensure that this agent can only be accessed from your application and nowhere else. Then it's also based in our agent ID. And now we can close this file. Then let's have a look at adding this agent to our application. The first thing we need to do is to install the 11labs library. So I'm going to close the session, and then in the 11labs documentation, we can find the React SDK page to see the installation instructions. So I'm going to use pnpm, so I'll simply copy this command and then run it in my terminal. Now that we've installed the 11labs SDK, we can start up the dev server again. And now at the top of the code, we can run import something from the 11labs React library. And what we want to grab is the use conversation hook. Then within our component, we can initialize that hook by creating a variable. I'll just call it conversation, which is equal to use conversation like so. And now we can actually grab two objects from this hook. We'll grab something from conversation. And what we want to grab is the status and is speaking. So status will simply tell us if we are connected to the 11labs agent in the back end. And we can use is speaking to determine if the agent is currently speaking. When opening this application, we need to ask the user for permission to use their microphone. So let's start with that first. Below this handler functions, I'm going to use use effect to ask the user for permission to use the microphone. We will add the logic in a second. And we won't add any dependencies as we only want this use effect to run once when the page has finished loading. Let's create a new function called request mic permission. And this is an async function. And within this function, we'll attempt to get permission to use the microphone. So we'll call await, then we'll call the navigator function in the browser. And on navigator, we can reference the media devices. And on this, we've got a method called get user media. And within this method, we can pass an object where we can set audio to true. So if the user permits access, this logic will simply continue. If they deny access, this will actually raise an exception. So to handle that exception, let's wrap this code in a try catch block. So I'll simply move this into the try block. And in the catch block, it's simply console log error accessing microphone, followed by the content of the error object itself, I want to keep track of this permission in state. And I also want to keep track of any error messages in state as well. So at the top of this function, let's create a few state variables. So first, let's create a state for as permission. And let's also create a set function called set has permission, which is equal to use state and the default value of false. This means that once a user permits access to their microphone, we can call set as permission to true. Otherwise, it will just remain as false. Let's also create state for the error message. So let's create a new state called error message with the set function called set error message, which is equal to use state with the default value of an empty string. And then let's go back to our catch block. And just below the console log, I'll call set error message. And we'll pass in error as error dot message. So this will simply grab the actual string text from this error object. And lastly, it's also called this request mic permission function in our use state. And when we refresh this, we will be asked to permit access to the microphone. So I'll select allow while visiting the site. Now let's implement this start conversation button. So in our component, we've got this button that we can use to start the conversation and it is hooked up to the handle start conversation function. Now if we jump over to this function, we can add our logic over here. Now to start a conversation with 11Labs SDK, we can simply create a new variable called conversation ID, which is equal to oh wait, then we can call our conversation object, which we instantiated over here. And on this object, we have a method called start session. This method takes in an object as input. And the only property we want to pass in at this stage is the agent ID, which we saved in our environment variables. So to access it, we can call process dot env dot next underscore public underscore 11Labs underscore agent underscore ID. And TypeScript is simply saying that this value could be undefined. So I'll simply add an exclamation mark to remove this error as we do expect the variable to be available. Then it's also console log, something like started conversation. And it's also write out the conversation ID. So back in our app, we can open up the console and let's hit start conversation. Greetings, traveler. How can I assist you on your quest? We can see that the conversation has started. And we can also see our unique conversation ID. Ah, yes, I see you're making progress on your quest. Now we just need some way to stop the conversation as well. So let's implement that logic. So let's scroll down to this button where we start a conversation and just above it, let's add another button. In fact, we can actually just copy this button as they do look identical. So let's copy this space to just above it. Let's change this text to end conversation. Then for this button, it's also add a variant and this variance value will be destructive. And for the handler function, we'll actually call handle and conversation. Let's remove this disable property. Then for the icon, let's change it from Mike to Mike off like so. And if we look at the application, we can see the end conversation button with this different icon. Now we also want to conditionally show these buttons. So if we're not connected to 11 labs, we can start a conversation or if we are connected, then we'll show end conversation instead. So just above this button, let's add some JavaScript code. And what we'll do is we'll check if the status is equal to connected and we'll add a question mark. So if you are connected, we'll show something otherwise, we'll show something else. So what do we want to show when we are connected? Well, we want to show this button to end the conversation. So let's cut this code and paste it into these parentheses. And now if we go back to our app, we will initially see start conversation and let's click on start. Greetings, traveler. How can I assist you on your quest? And now we're able to end the conversation as well. Ah, so you've finally found all 11 gemstones. That's quite a feat, traveler. Now let's give it a spin in the app. So let's start the conversation. Greetings, traveler. How can I assist you on your quest? And let's try ending it. Perfect. At this point, we've actually done it. We've added a real time conversational agent to our application. But we're not done yet, though, as I do want to show you one of the most important settings that you should add to your application as well. So in the toggle mute function, we can call conversation dot set volume. And within set volume, we can pass in an object. And in this object, you have this volume property. And now you can set any value between zero and one. So what we can do is simply create a new state called muted with a set muted function, which is equal to use state and the default value or false. Then let's go to this button, which we can use to toggle mute. And what I'll actually do is copy this line, because I do want to show a different icon if we are currently muted. Now the last piece of advice that I do want to give you is to protect your agent from being embedded into other applications. Ideally, we will now deploy this app to production, which will give us some sort of URL endpoint. And we can tell 11 labs that only that URL is allowed to access this agent. To do that, we can go back to 11 labs, then on our agent, we can go to security. And within the allow list, we can add the domains that should be allowed to access this agent. more_vertModel Thoughts Developing the StructureI've been working on structuring the YouTube transcript data. My focus is on creating a JSON output. I'm prioritizing summary and keyPoints fields. I will also incorporate aiConcepts soon. I'm aiming for a clear and usable format for experts in the field.Adding Technical DetailsI've expanded the JSON structure, now including technicalDetails, visualContextFlags, and a list of aiConcepts. I'm starting to visualize how to integrate the ElevenLabs conversational AI into a Next.js app, using the React SDK and handling real-time voice interaction and state management. The key technical steps, including setting up the Next.js app, creating an agent in the ElevenLabs dashboard, and using the useConversation hook, are also clearer now.Expanding Security ConsiderationsI'm now focusing on the crucial security implications. I've realized domain allow-listing is essential to protect Agent IDs. This aligns with a deeper dive into ElevenLabs' security, which is a critical aspect for public-facing applications. I'm also considering additional security best practices.Integrating Knowledge BasesI'm now integrating Knowledge Bases (RAG) and Agentic State Management into the architecture, while also considering function calling capabilities. I'm deep diving into the technical details, specifically the use of Next.js, the ElevenLabs React SDK, and the useConversation hook. I'm also planning to utilize navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia for audio input, and Shadcn UI (referred to as "chat cn") for the user interface. I also want to make use of environment variables with .env.local , while developing using pnpm / npm, and applying React useEffect and useState hooks. I've also identified key visual context flags from transcript descriptions to better implement those components.Focusing on ImplementationI'm now fully immersed in the coding phase, specifically on implementing the volume/mute controls for audio feedback. I'm also actively configuring security allow-lists within ElevenLabs. I'm meticulously validating that the project structure aligns with the defined specifications, confirming all of the required criteria is met. Expand to view model thoughts  chevron_right

editmore_vert{"summary": "This video demonstrates how to integrate a real-time, human-like AI voice agent into a Next.js application using the ElevenLabs React SDK. It covers the end-to-end process from configuring an agent in the ElevenLabs dashboard to implementing session controls and microphone permissions in code.","keyPoints": ["ElevenLabs provides a conversational AI platform currently in beta that allows for low-latency voice interactions.","Integration is simplified through a dedicated React SDK and the useConversation hook.","AI agents can be customized with specific system prompts, knowledge bases, and tool-calling capabilities.","Security is handled by adding production domains to an allow-list within the ElevenLabs dashboard.","The implementation requires handling browser-level microphone permissions using the Media Devices API."],"aiConcepts": ["Conversational AI","Low-latency speech-to-speech","LLM (Large Language Model) Providers","Knowledge Bases / RAG","Tool Calling","Natural Language Processing","Conversational State Management"],"technicalDetails": ["Next.js","ElevenLabs React SDK (@11labs/react)","useConversation hook","navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia","Environment variables (NEXT_PUBLIC prefix)","Shadcn UI","Tailwind CSS","pnpm/npm"],"visualContextFlags": [{"timestamp":
